questions.question: What causes timing side-channel leakage in polynomial comparison?

options.text: The polynomial coefficients are too large
Early-exit when a difference is found reveals the position
The comparison algorithm is incorrect
The processor cache is too small

questions.explanation: Early-exit comparison returns as soon as a difference is found. An attacker can measure timing differences to learn where polynomials differ, potentially revealing secret information through the position of differences.

questions.question: How does cache timing attack work against NTT operations?

options.text: The cache becomes corrupted during NTT
Secret-dependent table indices cause different cache hit/miss patterns
The NTT algorithm is fundamentally insecure
Cache attacks only work against AES, not PQC

questions.explanation: When NTT accesses twiddle factor tables, the index may depend on secret data. Cache hits (fast) vs. misses (slow) create measurable timing differences that reveal the access pattern and thus secret information.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Boolean masking in side-channel defense?

options.text: To encrypt the secret key
To split secrets into random shares that hide the original value
To make the algorithm run faster
To reduce memory usage

questions.explanation: Boolean masking splits a secret value x into shares (x₀, x₁) where x = x₀ ⊕ x₁. Each share individually reveals no information about x, protecting against power analysis that correlates with intermediate values.

questions.question: What ML-DSA operation is particularly vulnerable to timing attacks?

options.text: Key generation
Verification
Rejection sampling during signing
Signature encoding

questions.explanation: ML-DSA uses rejection sampling during signing where the number of iterations correlates with secret key coefficients. Variable iteration count creates timing leakage that can reveal information about the secret key.

questions.question: How does execution shuffling defend against SPA attacks?

options.text: It encrypts the power consumption
It randomizes operation order so traces cannot be aligned
It reduces total power consumption
It increases the clock speed

questions.explanation: Shuffling randomizes the order in which operations are performed. Since SPA requires aligning multiple traces to identify patterns, random execution order makes it much harder to correlate power measurements.

questions.question: What is a constant-time table lookup implementation?

options.text: A lookup that uses hardware acceleration
A lookup that accesses all table entries regardless of the actual index
A lookup that caches all values in registers
A lookup that uses smaller tables

questions.explanation: Constant-time table lookup accesses ALL entries and uses masking to select the correct value. This ensures identical cache access patterns regardless of the secret index being looked up.

questions.question: Power analysis attacks require physical access to the target device.
questions.explanation: Power analysis attacks require measuring the electrical power consumption of a device during cryptographic operations. This typically requires physical access to the device or its power supply, though sophisticated attackers may use EM emissions from a distance.
